rationale_for_baseline_tier: The reaction is confined to the skin (urticaria/flushing/dermatographism) with no airway, circulatory or GI involvement, so it is not anaphylaxis and does not warrant emergency transport. It is best managed with antihistamine up-dosing, cooling and a very low-threshold anaphylaxis safety-net (T2 self-care + telehealth advice). Because the patient has MCAS and lives alone, ensuring an adrenaline autoinjector/action plan is essential. Any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature means adrenaline and 000 (T5). Minimum viable pass is T2; a reflexive ambulance for isolated urticaria is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Throat/tongue swelling, voice change, wheeze, difficulty breathing, dizziness/faintness, or vomiting
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Use adrenaline if available and call 000 immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate evolving anaphylaxis requiring adrenaline and emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your tongue or throat swells, your voice changes, you wheeze or can't breathe properly, or you feel faint — use your adrenaline pen if you have one and call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No adrenaline autoinjector available in a patient with MCAS living alone
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange prompt GP review for an autoinjector and action plan
escalation_edges.rationale: An isolated MCAS patient should have emergency adrenaline cover.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you don't have an adrenaline pen, arrange to see your GP promptly to get one and an action plan.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash and distress not settling despite antihistamine up-dosing and cooling
escalation_edges.rationale: Refractory severe urticaria may need in-person treatment (e.g. short steroid course).
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the hives and itching don't settle despite extra antihistamines and cooling, arrange to be seen the same day.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure an adrenaline autoinjector/action plan and advise checking in with someone; lower threshold to escalate given no one is present.
Flags future anaphylaxis risk and the need for a daily antihistamine regimen and specialist review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a skin-only mast cell flare, not a dangerous whole-body allergic reaction — your breathing, throat and blood pressure are unaffected. Take extra doses of your non-sedating antihistamine, cool down (cool shower, loose cotton), and avoid heat and friction. Make sure you have an adrenaline pen and action plan.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with telehealth follow-up; use adrenaline + 000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use 000 immediately for any airway/breathing/circulation/GI features; otherwise manage at home and review with your GP.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the heat-triggered isolated cutaneous flare, cetirizine failure, and whether an adrenaline autoinjector/action plan is in place.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassurance without an anaphylaxis screen/safety-net or autoinjector check, missing evolving anaphylaxis in an isolated patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The reaction progresses to airway involvement overnight and no adrenaline is available.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for an isolated cutaneous flare with no systemic features, over-medicalising a manageable urticaria.
